Event: Holds Storm's End under siege conditions despite starvation
Explanation: His endurance keeps Robert’s rebellion viable and his men alive, gaining him reputation and survival.

Event: Chooses to support Robert over his lawful Targaryen king in the rebellion
Explanation: He sacrifices safety for family loyalty, helping Robert win while undermining the prior legal ruler.

Event: Nearly starves to death during the Siege of Storm’s End rather than surrender
Explanation: He incurs severe personal harm to benefit his brother’s rebellion and men.

Event: Distributes scarce food to wife and soldiers before eating after the siege is lifted
Explanation: He forgoes privilege to feed others first, earning deep loyalty at modest personal cost.

Event: Accepts Dragonstone as “reassignment” and broods over slight
Explanation: His resentment over the posting eats at him without yielding tangible gain.

Event: Frequently berates Renly for not being a warrior
Explanation: His bullying vents frustration and may reinforce his authority while damaging their relationship.

Event: Refuses to negotiate genuine peace with Renly and Robb because he sees them as thieves
Explanation: Holding to his rigid claim blocks potentially stabilizing alliances and prolongs conflict.

Event: Uses Melisandre’s blood magic to assassinate his brother Renly
Explanation: He removes a rival and gains troops in the short term at great moral and political cost.

Event: Shrugs off Renly’s death as acceptable collateral at first
Explanation: Emotionally distancing himself helps his focus but deepens others’ hostility to him.

Event: Insists on broadcasting Joffrey’s true parentage to many rather than keep it secret
Explanation: Openly denouncing Joffrey clarifies his claim and weakens opponents while increasing war risk.

Event: Learns of Mance’s invasion and decides to march north to the Wall
Explanation: He redirects his limited forces to confront the existential threat, benefiting all at some personal strategic cost.

Event: Goes to the Iron Bank, frankly admitting his weak position to secure funds
Explanation: His brutal honesty wins crucial financial backing that sustains his campaign.

Event: Holds up in Dragonstone and refuses to see anyone except Melisandre
Explanation: Withdrawing into isolation erodes alliances and gains no compensating benefit.

Event: Allows Melisandre to burn “heretics” freely after Blackwater
Target: Dissenters and his own support base
Explanation: Terrorizes and shrinks his following while tying himself closer to an unpopular cult.

Event: Nearly strangles Melisandre after Blackwater in a fury over defeat
Explanation: An impulsive attack risks losing his main magical ally without solving his strategic problems.

Event: Decides to use blood magic to kill Gendry for king’s blood
Explanation: He seeks magical advantage at the cost of murdering kin, trading ethics for a speculative edge.

Event: Comes to Davos about Gendry instead of killing the boy immediately
Explanation: He implicitly invites dissent, which opens a path to spare Gendry and keep Davos.

Event: Releases Davos from prison to argue against sacrificing Gendry
Explanation: He restores a loyal advisor and empowers him to avert an atrocity, strengthening his moral core.

Event: Spares Gendry after Davos’s persuasion
Explanation: He forgoes dubious magical gain to preserve kin and honor, improving legitimacy and trust.

Event: Seriously contemplates burning Gendry earlier for power
Explanation: The consideration itself reflects willingness to trade kin for potential advantage.

Event: Tells Davos he doesn’t really want the throne but must claim it from duty
Explanation: Framing ambition as duty steadies his resolve while anchoring it in a larger rationale.

Event: Burns dissenters alive as a regular tool of rule
Explanation: Terror tactics consolidate short-term obedience while poisoning long-term support.

Event: Makes Davos his Hand despite Davos’s low birth
Explanation: He elevates competence and loyalty over class, gaining an exceptionally faithful and honest Hand.

Event: Leads the fleet and storming party personally at Blackwater
Explanation: Frontline leadership inspires men and nearly wins the city, though it exposes him to risk.

Event: Refuses to retreat at Blackwater until physically forced back
Explanation: His stubborn courage sacrifices men and himself for a now-unwinnable position.

Event: Issues the terse rousing command “Come with me and take this city!” at Blackwater
Explanation: A clear, brave lead-from-the-front order galvanizes troops toward a concrete objective.

Event: Holds to burning Mance unless he bends the knee
Explanation: He seeks submission and northern strength but alienates wildlings who might have joined more freely.

Event: Refuses Melisandre’s initial suggestion to sacrifice Shireen
Explanation: He protects his daughter and his humanity, preserving family and moral authority.

Event: Takes Selyse and Shireen with him to the Wall due to rapists in the Watch
Explanation: Bringing them along modestly increases risk but removes them from an unsafe environment.

Event: Treats Shireen kindly and protects her from Selyse’s harshness
Explanation: His rare warmth and defense give her security and emotional benefit without hurting his cause.

Event: Calls in every healer to save Shireen from grayscale
Explanation: He invests heavily to cure her, gaining a living heir and deepening their bond.

Event: Decides to march on Winterfell rather than wintering at Castle Black
Explanation: High-risk offensive aims to dislodge Boltons and secure the North, with mixed eventual payoff.

Event: Assassinates Renly with a shadow to seize his army and remove rival
Explanation: Short-term gain in forces comes at the cost of trust and aligning himself with dark magic.

Event: Allows Melisandre to continue advising him despite knowing her dangers
Explanation: Reliance on her power helps in crises but increasingly corrodes his principles and alliances.

Event: Invokes Ned Stark’s memory to Jon and others when seeking northern support
Explanation: Tying himself to Ned’s legacy bolsters legitimacy and moral framing of his cause.

Event: Offers to legitimize Jon Snow as Jon Stark and make him Lord of Winterfell
Explanation: He trades a title for a vital Stark ally, which would strengthen both men if accepted.

Event: Respects Jon’s refusal to accept legitimacy and remain in the Night’s Watch
Explanation: Honoring Jon’s oath builds mutual respect and leaves future cooperation possible.

Event: Tells Sam about the dragonglass on Dragonstone and urges him to keep reading
Explanation: He pushes Sam toward knowledge that later becomes crucial against the White Walkers.

Event: Decides to abandon southern campaign and march north to defend the Wall
Explanation: He sacrifices near-term claim-building to confront the existential threat to all.

Event: Executes Mance Rayder for refusing to bend the knee
Explanation: The execution asserts royal authority but wastes a charismatic leader whose cooperation could help the realm.

Event: Allows the wildlings an offer of subject-rights if they kneel and accept his rule
Explanation: He offers marginalized people legal status in exchange for loyalty, a mutually beneficial if tough bargain.

Event: Expresses disgust at the Red Wedding and vows to execute Roose and Walder
Explanation: Condemning treachery aligns him with justice and can rally honorable support.

Event: Pivots his forces to perform a pincer attack on the wildling host at the Wall
Explanation: He annihilates a massive threat with minimal losses, saving the North and Night’s Watch.

Event: Burns multiple followers and nobles who oppose him after setbacks
Explanation: He terrorizes and shrinks his already small base without achieving proportional gains.

Event: Encourages Brienne to execute him and tells her to do her duty
Explanation: He accepts death, losing his own life while granting Brienne closure and reinforcing her code.

Event: Freely confesses to Brienne that he assassinated Renly
Explanation: Truthfulness earns him no mercy but gives Brienne moral clarity and preserves his own integrity.

Event: Draws his sword and advances to face the charging Bolton army despite being doomed
Explanation: His defiance preserves honor and inspires some, but tactically it only hastens their deaths.

Event: Orders or allows Shireen to be burned alive to gain magical advantage
Explanation: He destroys his beloved daughter and shatters his support; the supposed benefit never materializes.

Event: Says “Forgive me” to Shireen before her sacrifice
Explanation: The admission eases his conscience slightly but offers Shireen no rescue and deepens his self-loathing.

Event: Continues the march on Winterfell after half his army deserts and wife suicides
Explanation: Pressing a hopeless attack ensures slaughter without realistic chance of victory.

Event: Allows Melisandre to burn people for the Lord of Light after Blackwater defeat
Explanation: He shores up faith in his chosen god at the cost of alienating potential allies.

Event: Stares into the flames and adopts the identity of “Prince That Was Promised”
Explanation: Believing the prophecy strengthens resolve but locks him into disastrous fanatic choices.

Event: Remains determined to claim the throne because law says it is his
Explanation: His legalism sustains war that destabilizes the kingdom for an outcome centered on his right.

Event: Publicly insists “The Iron Throne is mine. All those that deny that are my foes.”
Explanation: This declaration rallies his followers but hardens everyone else into implacable enemies.

Event: Refuses to compromise or soften his personality to gain allies
Explanation: His rigidity costs him critical support without giving a corresponding strategic edge.

Event: Leads from the front again against the Boltons despite having no cavalry or hope
Explanation: His personal courage can’t offset the tactical disaster, leading to near-total annihilation.

Event: Allows Melisandre to leave camp after the desertions and Shireen’s death
Explanation: Letting his last magical ally go deprives him of potential tools without regaining trust or numbers.

Event: Ignored Stormlands bannermen earlier rather than courting them effectively
Explanation: His failure to win natural bannermen harms his cause more than his pride gains.

Event: Publicly admits guilt in assassinating Renly when asked by Brienne
Explanation: Admission brings deserved execution but upholds truth and lets Brienne act without doubt.
